name: Mason Ruder
occupation: Director of Institutional Advancement, Hays Catholic Schools; Certified Public Manager
education: Thomas More Prep-Marian High School (Class of 2010); Fort Hays State University (Political Leadership and Public Service); University of Kansas Certified Public Manager (CPM) program
family: Wife Tori; daughters Ryleigh and Isabelle. Son of Vernon Ruder Jr. (Ellis County Treasurer) and Ruth Ruder (USD 489 School Board member). Siblings: Blake and Erin.
whoTheyAre:
Mason Ruder is the current mayor of Hays and a city commissioner who has served since 2019, when he was first elected as a Fort Hays State University student. He was the youngest city commissioner in Hays history. Born and raised in Hays, he graduated from Thomas More Prep-Marian in 2010 and later earned a degree in Political Leadership and Public Service from FHSU. He also completed the Certified Public Manager program through the University of Kansas.

Ruder spent about five years as Ellis County's Environmental Services Director, where he helped shape the county's Comprehensive Plan and Zoning Regulations. In December 2022, he returned to his roots at Hays Catholic Schools as the Director of Institutional Advancement, leading fundraising efforts for Holy Family Elementary and Thomas More Prep-Marian. He lives in Hays with his wife Tori and their two daughters, Ryleigh and Isabelle.

His family is deeply woven into Hays public life. His father, Vernon Ruder Jr., serves as Ellis County Treasurer. His mother, Ruth Ruder, sits on the USD 489 school board. Mason himself has said that faith and family are the foundation of his life, and his career choices reflect that. He left a position in Kansas City and moved back to Hays specifically to raise his family in the community where he grew up.
theirRecord:
As a city commissioner and now mayor, Ruder has focused on long-term infrastructure, housing, and youth engagement. His signature initiative is the Youth Leadership Advisory Board, which he created to give Hays High and TMP-Marian students a direct voice in city government. Seven students serve on the board, working on projects ranging from mock elections to mental health awareness and fire explorer programs.

Ruder has been one of the most vocal supporters of the R9 Ranch water transfer project, which he calls a generational infrastructure investment. He traveled to Washington, D.C., with the city manager and city attorney to lobby federal representatives for R9 funding, and he testified before the Kansas House Local Government Committee in January 2026 to defend the project. He has also supported housing development through Reinvestment Housing Incentive Districts and backed downtown quality-of-life improvements, including a permanent stage in Municipal Park and the city's common consumption area.

On the other side of the ledger, Ruder cast the sole dissenting vote against the 2020 mask mandate extension, splitting 3-1 from his fellow commissioners during a heated period of the COVID-19 pandemic. He was the only commissioner to vote no while the other three faced public backlash, including vandalism, for voting yes. Whether voters see that vote as principled independence or a misstep depends on their own views, but it is the clearest public disagreement attached to his time on the commission.
whatYouShouldKnow: Mason Ruder is part of a family that holds three elected positions in Hays-area government at the same time. His father Vernon Ruder Jr. is the Ellis County Treasurer, and his mother Ruth Ruder is a USD 489 school board member. No evidence of impropriety has been found, but the concentration of one family across city, county, and school board leadership is unusual and worth knowing.

Ruder was the youngest city commissioner in Hays history when first elected in 2019 as an FHSU student. He was re-elected as the top vote-getter in 2023 and elected mayor by his fellow commissioners in January 2026.
He cast the sole dissenting vote against the 2020 COVID-19 mask mandate extension. The other three commissioners voted yes and faced vandalism for their decision. Ruder was the only 'no' vote.
He created the Youth Leadership Advisory Board, a seven-member panel of Hays High and TMP-Marian students who advise the city commission on community issues.

Ruder is a leading champion of the R9 Ranch water transfer project, which he has called a once-in-a-generation infrastructure priority. He testified in Topeka and lobbied in Washington, D.C., to advance the project.
His day job is Director of Institutional Advancement for Hays Catholic Schools, where he leads fundraising for Holy Family Elementary and Thomas More Prep-Marian. He is a 2010 TMP-Marian graduate.

Ruder also works as a Business Services Specialist at the Northwest Kansas Economic Innovation Center (NWKEICI), where his supervisor is Michael Berges, who serves as an Ellis County Commissioner. This means a county commissioner is the professional boss of the city's mayor.

He wrote a 9/11 anniversary editorial published by the Kansas Press Association in September 2025, headlined 'Forgetting the lesson of 9/11 is the danger we face now.' The piece was also published in the Hays Daily News.
whereTheyWorship:
Mason Ruder is a member of Immaculate Heart of Mary Parish in Hays, a Roman Catholic church in the Diocese of Salina. His connection to the Catholic faith runs deep. He graduated from Thomas More Prep-Marian, the Catholic high school in Hays, and later returned to work for Hays Catholic Schools as the director of institutional advancement. In his own words, 'Faith and family have always provided a solid foundation.'

His faith is not just a personal matter but is woven into his professional life and public identity. He fundraises for the same Catholic school system that educated him, and his parish membership at Immaculate Heart of Mary is part of a broader pattern of deep involvement in Catholic community life in Hays.

church.name: Immaculate Heart of Mary Parish
church.details: Located at 1805 Vine St, Hays, KS 67601. Ruder is identified as a member in his Hays Catholic Schools profile.

quotes.text: Faith and family have always provided a solid foundation.
It was about getting youth involved in our community a little more and having that direct connection to the city commission and really influence change.
True government means disagreeing and working things out.
Let's keep growing Hays. We have a lot of work to do.
Every day is now an R9 Ranch Day.
